title: Stool, urine, sweat: the three wastes (malas)
excerpt: Your body produces waste every single day, and Ayurveda says that is not something to ignore. The three malas, or waste products, are signals your constitution sends you about how well your digestion and metabolism are really working.
at_a_glance: The three malas are stool, urine, and sweat: healthy elimination of all three is a core sign of balance in Ayurveda.
Classical texts say suppressing natural urges disturbs apana vata and can lead to dryness and discomfort over time.
Warm water on waking, regular mealtimes, and a daily walk are the simplest everyday supports for healthy elimination.

body.heading: a daily conversation your body is having
paragraphs: Most of us glance at the toilet and move on without a second thought. But in Ayurveda, what leaves your body every morning is as informative as what you put into it. The classical texts call these outputs the three malas, a Sanskrit word meaning wastes or impurities. The three are purisha (stool), mutra (urine), and sweda (sweat). Collectively they are the body's way of clearing what is no longer useful, and when they flow freely and look the way they should, it is a reliable sign that your digestion and metabolism are in good order.

The idea is not exotic. It mirrors what modern physiology says about elimination. What Ayurveda adds is a detailed picture of what healthy output looks like, and a framework for reading the signs when something is off.

body.heading: what the classical texts actually say
paragraphs: Caraka Samhita describes the malas as natural byproducts of ahara paka, the transformation of food by the digestive fire (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The text draws a clear line between the sara, or essence that nourishes the body's tissues, and the kitta, the residue that must be expelled. Suppressing this expulsion, the text warns, disturbs the doshas and eventually creates disease (Caraka Samhita, Vimanasthana).

Ashtanga Hridaya, the compact eighth-century digest that synthesised earlier knowledge, carries the same point forward. It describes the mala-carrying channels (srotas) that govern elimination and notes that obstructed or excessive flow in these channels is a primary site where imbalance takes hold (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). Sushruta Samhita, which approaches the body more through a surgical lens, also counts healthy elimination among the signs of a person in good health, listing regular stool, clear urine, and appropriate sweating as markers of a balanced state (Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana).

Healthy purisha, according to these references, should be well-formed, passed without strain, and neither too hard nor too loose. Mutra should be pale yellow and clear, passed a comfortable number of times through the day without burning or urgency. Sweda should emerge in response to heat or effort and leave the skin feeling clean rather than sticky or odorous beyond what is normal for the season. Deviation in any of these directions is a prompt to look more carefully at what you are eating, how you are sleeping, and whether you are managing stress.

body.heading: simple habits that keep elimination healthy
paragraphs: The practical guidance in Ayurveda around the malas comes down to a few principles that map easily onto daily life. The first is regularity. Going to bed and waking at consistent times helps the body set a reliable rhythm for elimination. Most people find that a trip to the toilet happens naturally within an hour of waking when they rise early and eat their main meal in the afternoon.

Warm water first thing in the morning is one of the most consistently mentioned supports across the classical texts and their later commentaries. A glass or two of warm water on an empty stomach helps stimulate bowel movement gently without relying on caffeine. Adding a small squeeze of lemon is a common folk extension of this idea, though the classical texts themselves speak more broadly about warm liquids in the morning.

Food choices matter a great deal. Meals that are warm, cooked, and moderately oily support stool formation better than dry, cold, or excessively raw food, especially during the cooler months. Adequate water intake through the day keeps urine appropriately diluted. Physical activity, even a 20-minute walk, supports sweating and helps prevent the sluggishness that leads to constipation.

One habit that Ayurvedic texts explicitly caution against is suppressing natural urges. Holding in stool or urine when the urge is present is said to disturb the downward-moving energy called apana vata, which governs elimination. Over time this can create dryness, irregular bowel habits, and lower abdominal discomfort (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: Changes in stool, urine, or sweat are usually temporary and tied to what you ate or how much you slept. But some signs deserve prompt medical attention. See a qualified doctor if you notice blood in your stool or urine, persistent dark or foul-smelling urine, a complete inability to pass stool or urine, sudden and unexplained changes in your elimination pattern that last more than a week or two, or sweating that is severe, happens mainly at night, or is accompanied by fever and weight loss.

This article is general health information rooted in classical Ayurvedic literature. It is not a diagnosis and is not a substitute for evaluation by a medical doctor or a qualified vaidya. If you are concerned about your symptoms, please consult a healthcare professional.

try_this_today.title: Warm water on waking
try_this_today.description: Drink one or two glasses of plain warm water before tea or coffee. This gently stimulates the bowel and helps set a regular morning rhythm.

try_this_today.title: Do not suppress natural urges
try_this_today.description: When the urge to pass stool or urine arises, respond to it promptly. The texts are unusually firm on this point: habitual suppression is one of the more reliable ways to disturb elimination over time.

try_this_today.title: Check your urine colour
try_this_today.description: Pale yellow and clear is the target. Consistently dark urine through the day usually means you need more water. Very pale or almost colourless may mean you are overhydrating, which is less common but worth noting.

try_this_today.title: Eat a warm, cooked main meal
try_this_today.description: One well-cooked meal with a small amount of good fat (ghee or oil) supports stool formation better than a diet heavy in dry, raw, or processed food.

words_to_know.definition: Waste or impurity; the residue left after digestion that the body must expel
Stool; the solid waste expelled by the large intestine
Urine; the liquid waste filtered by the kidneys
Sweat; the waste expelled through the skin during heat or exertion
The downward-moving sub-type of vata that governs elimination and reproductive functions
The residue or waste fraction separated out during the digestion of food

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Sushruta Samhita, Sutrasthana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
